title: Module: clinical-trial phases (I-IV) & what each can/can't show
context: A common advocacy error is treating a phase-I 'response rate' or a phase-II safety signal as evidence of clinical benefit. This module teaches patient advocates what each trial phase is designed to establish — dose safety and tolerability (I), preliminary efficacy signals (II), comparative efficacy against standard of care (III), post-market surveillance (IV) — and critically, what each phase cannot prove. This is patient-facing educational content requiring dual high-tier sign-off.
objective: Author an original, plain-language educational module on clinical-trial phases I through IV, explaining the purpose, typical design, and the specific questions each phase can and cannot answer, with emphasis on the limits advocates should understand when reading phase-I or phase-II results.
acceptanceCriteria: Covers phases I through IV: purpose, typical design features (dose escalation in I; endpoint types in II; randomisation and comparator in III; post-market safety in IV).

Explicitly addresses what each phase cannot show: phase I is not an efficacy signal; phase II response rates are not equivalent to phase III survival benefit; phase III results apply to the studied population and may not generalise.
Addresses basket trials, platform trials, and adaptive designs as increasingly common phase II/III variants — briefly, with plain-language explanation.
Cites FDA/EMA guidance on clinical-trial phases and authoritative oncology methodology sources; all citations resolve to sources.yaml entries with compatible allowedUse.
Written in plain language; no unexplained jargon without a glossary anchor.
Carries a persistent 'education only — not medical advice' notice and passes the advice-language checklist (task 002).
Passes the licence + provenance build gate and CI.
Signed off by a credentialed oncologist (recorded in the review ledger): clinical framing is correct and nothing reads as treatment guidance.
Signed off by an experienced patient advocate (recorded in the review ledger): content is usable and does not stray into advice.
Signed off by a methodology reviewer: phase descriptions and limitations are methodologically accurate.
resources: PLAN.md (§ Goals — Goal 1; § Dependencies — FDA/EMA endpoint guidance)
output: A plain-language educational module (content/modules/trial-phases.md) committed to the repo, covering phases I–IV and their limits, provenance-cited, CI-green, and carrying dual high-tier sign-off plus methodology review.
